words: This conversation with Lucille Riddell Genevan, took place at her home in
Lewiston, Idaho on November 11, 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: SS
words: When did you first think you were interested in becoming a nurse?

speaker: LD
words: Oh, I always wanted to be a nurse, from the time I was a little girl. We

speaker: LD
words: lived close to a hospital in Warren, Minnesota. And my mother said I

speaker: LD
words: use to follow those nurses down the street every chance I got. I guess I

speaker: LD
words: admired their uniforms, that's what I liked. So I always wanted to be a

speaker: LD
words: nurse, as long as I can remember Isanted to be a nurse. Well, I wanted

speaker: LD
words: to go to Minneapolis and take my training, because Ithought I'd have so

speaker: LD
words: many more advantages, bigger schoola and everything. But my mother didn't

speaker: LD
words: want me to be a nurse. She wanted me to be a teacher. And I told her I didn't

speaker: LD
words: think I was smart enough to be a teacher. See, I only had one year of high school,

speaker: LD
words: that's all I had. And she said,"Well, you can go on to school and be a

speaker: LD
words: teacher." You know, nurses aren't thought much of. So, my older sister,

speaker: LD
words: who was 17 years older than I, she was just like a mother said, "Why don't

speaker: LD
words: I take her?? 'Cause I as only 16."Why don't I take her with me and take care

speaker: LD
words: of her?" And she only lived two blocks from this small hospital. I got very

speaker: LD
words: good training there, and then I had by last 6 months in Chicago.

speaker: LD

speaker: LD

speaker: LD
words: Then I came from there to Bovill.

speaker: LD

speaker: LD
words: I really don't think so. The idea of them having to bathe men and all.

speaker: LD
words: My mother didn't like it. She was a person that didn't go along with that

speaker: LD
words: at all. And she thought it would be so much nicer if I would be a teacher.

speaker: LD
words: I would be looked up to so much more if I were a teacher. She said, another

speaker: LD
words: thing, I was awfully hard work, which it was. hard work and she

speaker: LD
words: just didn't want me to be a nurse. She thought that I could do better than

words: being a nurse. I look at the wages the nurses make now. Course, we didn t

speaker: LD

words: go home then, why should you run up that big bill? And you know, those

speaker: LD

words: the next day, taking care of themselves and going to the bathroom and taking

words: their own baths and everything like that. What if they faint or something

words: like that? Nobody to look after 'em. those nurses don't pay any attention

speaker: LD
words: to 'em.

speaker: LD

speaker: LD
